--- Page 1 ---
Queueing models with random resetting
DONGZHOU HUANG‚ąó, GUODONG PANG‚ąó‚ąó, IZABELLA STUHL‚Ä†, AND YURI SUHOV$
Abstract. We introduce and study some queueing models with random resetting, including
Markovian and non‚ÄďMarkovian models. The Markovian models include M/M/‚ąě, M/M/r
and M/M/1+M queues with random resetting, in which a continuous-time Markov chain is
formulated, with transitions including a resetting to state zero in addition to arrivals and
services. We explicitly characterize the stationary distributions of the queueing processes
in these models by using parting balance equations. Although the stationary distribution
for M/M/‚ąěqueue with resetting has been previously derived in the literature, we obtain
an alternative and more interpretable expression by a different approach. That provides
useful insights for the analysis of M/M/r and M/M/1+M queues with resetting under the
first-come first-served (FCFS) discipline.
The non‚ÄďMarkovian models include GI/GI/1, GI/GI/r and GI/GI/‚ąěqueues with random
resetting to state zero at arrival times. For GI/GI/1 and GI/GI/r queues under the FCFS
discipline, we introduce modified Lindley and Kiefer‚ÄďWolfowitz recursions, respectively. Using
an operator representation for these recursions, we characterize the stationary distributions
via convergent series, as solutions to the modified Wiener‚ÄďHopf equations. For GI/GI/‚ąě
queues with resettings, we utilize a version of the Kiefer‚ÄďWolfowitz recursion, and also
characterize the corresponding stationary distribution.
1. Introduction
In this paper we study some queueing models with random resetting, in which a queue
clears when resettings occurs. Such models have many applications in service systems where
machines or servers are subject to maintenance after some random time or periodically, or
where disruptions occur due to power loss or breaking down. There exists a substantial
literature on how to model such phenomena, including queues in random environments,
queues with disasters or catastrophes, and so on. In this work we aim to develop a number
of queueing models with random resetting in a unified manner, treating both Markovian and
non‚ÄďMarkovian models.
We start with classical Markovian models with random resetting, arising from the standard
M/M/‚ąě, M/M/r and M/M/1+M systems. The queueing process in these original models is
a birth‚Äďdeath process with jumps ¬Ī1, whose stationary distribution is known explicitly. In a
system with resetting, the queueing process is a continuous-time Markov chain (CTMC) with
an added hop to state 0. Consequently, the chain with resetting is no longer a birth‚Äďdeath
Date: November 27, 2025.
Key words and phrases. Queues, random resetting, stationary distribution, M/M/‚ąě, M/M/r, M/M/1+M,
GI/GI/1, GI/GI/r, GI/GI/‚ąě, modified Lindley recursion with random resetting, modified Kiefer‚ÄďWolfowitz
recursion with random resetting, modified Wiener‚ÄďHopf equation, resetting at arrival times.
1
arXiv:2505.00198v3  [math.PR]  25 Nov 2025


--- Page 2 ---
2
DONGZHOU HUANG, GUODONG PANG, IZABELLA STUHL, AND YURI SUHOV
process: here the transitions are characterized by arrival, departure and resetting rates.
Nevertheless, we are able to derive an explicit form for the stationary distribution.
The stationary distribution of M/M/‚ąěqueue with random resetting has been derived in
[7], in the context of models with catastrophes, where the author first derived the transient
distributions and then derived the stationary distributions by letting time go to infinity.
We provide an alternative (and more concise) expression for the stationary distribution of
the M/M/‚ąěmodel from [7], by a different approach; see Theorem 2.1 and Remark 2.4 for
comparison of our result from that in [7]. Indeed, we start with the partial balance equations
(PBEs) directly and derive an explicit expression for the generating function whose coefficients
lead to the stationary distributions with an analytically convenient integral representation,
instead of a double geometric summation in [7]. Furthermore, our expression for the stationary
distribution also provides an instructive interpretation, in terms of a Poisson-like structure
(see Remarks 2.1 and 2.2). Our method and result provide helpful insights in the analysis of
the M/M/r and M/M/1+M queues with random resettings.
For a classical M/M/r queue under the first-come first-served (FCFS) discipline, the
existence of a stationary distribution requires that the traffic intensity is less than one. It
turns out that for the M/M/r queues with random resetting under FCFS discipline, the
CTMC for the queueing process is always positive recurrent (even if the departure rate equals
0), and its stationary distribution can be written down explicitly; see Theorem 3.1. We take a
similar approach as in the study of M/M/‚ąěqueue with resetting, by using the PBEs directly
and a truncated generating function. However, the derivation of the stationary distribution
from the truncated generating function becomes much more challenging. The expression
of the stationary distribution involves the same integral representation as in that of the
M/M/‚ąěqueue with resetting, which is not surprising since the dynamics of the M/M/r
queue is the same as that of the M/M/‚ąěqueue when the state of the system is less than
r. Moreover, the form of the stationary distribution involves several series of constants that
satisfy a common recursive equation but with different boundary conditions (see Lemmas
2.1, 3.1 and 3.2). In particular, it exhibits a structure analogous to that of the classical
M/M/r queues, that is, when the state is less than r, it has a Poisson-like structure as that
in the M/M/‚ąěqueue with resetting, and when the state is larger than r, it possesses a
geometric-like structure. Moreover, when the resetting rate goes to zero, the form of the
stationary distribution coincides with that of the classical M/M/r queue (see Corollary 3.1).
The M/M/1 and M/M/2 models with resetting have been studied in [20] and [21], respectively,
with the same procedure mentioned above as in [7]. The result for the M/M/r queue with a
general number of servers r is novel, to the best of our knowledge.
We also study the M/M/1+M queue with random resetting under the FCFS discipline,
which has not been considered before. As in the previous models, we explicitly derive the
stationary distribution of the queueing process (see Theorem 4.2). We note that a special
case of the model with equal service and abandonment rates coincides with the M/M/‚ąě
model with random resetting. Hence, the stationary distribution of the M/M/‚ąěmodel with
resetting, together with that of the classical M/M/1+M queue, provides useful insight that
enables us to conjecture the stationary distribution of the M/M/1+M queue with random


--- Page 3 ---
3
resetting. We then establish that the proposed formula is indeed the unique stationary
distribution by showing that it satisfies the PBEs. In particular, it is verified that when the
resetting rate goes to zero, the form of the stationary distribution coincides with that of the
classical M/M/1+M queue (see Remark 4.1).
Next, we analyze non‚ÄďMarkovian queueing models with random resetting, starting with the
standard GI/GI/1 and GI/GI/r systems under the FCFS discipline and continuing with the
GI/GI/‚ąěsystem. For a standard GI/GI/1 queue, the Lindley recursion is fundamental in
studying properties of the delay/waiting-time distribution (see Section 5.1 for a brief review as
well as [2, Chapter X.1]). Similarly, for a standard GI/GI/r queue, it is the Kiefer‚ÄďWolfowitz
recursion that determines the delay/waiting‚Äďtime distribution (see Section 6.1). We study
the corresponding models where random resetting occurs at customers‚Äô arrival times, which
leads to modified Lindley and Kiefer‚ÄďWolfowitz recursions (see equations (5.4) and (6.7)).
For standard GI/GI/1 and GI/GI/r queues under the FCFS discipline, the positive
recurrence of the waiting‚Äďtime process requires that the traffic intensity is less than one, and
then the stationary distribution is characterized via the Wiener‚ÄďHopf equation. For these
models with random resetting, we show that the corresponding modified Lindley and the
Kiefer‚ÄďWolfowitz recursions generate positive recurrent waiting‚Äďtime processes, regardless of
whether their standard counterparts are positive recurrent or not. More importantly, the
modified Lindley and the Kiefer‚ÄďWolfowitz recursions can be conveniently represented in an
operator form (see equations (5.9) and (6.5)), from which we are able to express the stationary
distribution as a convergent series (see equations (5.11) and (6.9)-(iv)). As a byproduct, we
give an example of a GI/GI/1 queue with random resetting where the interarrival times are
dominated by the service times, but the waiting-time stationary distribution can be explicitly
expressed, see equation (5.13).
Finally, we consider GI/GI/‚ąěmodels with random resetting at arrival times. Here we
construct a recursion for the elapsed service times for the jobs in service, by adapting the
Kiefer‚ÄďWolfowitz recursion; we then use it to formulate the recursion for the GI/GI/‚ąěqueues
with resetting. As a result, we derive an explicit expression for the stationary distribution
(see Section 7).
A review of the literature. The models discussed in this paper are related to several streams
of the existing literature. First, these models are related to the stochastic clearing models
studied in [28, 27, 33], where an input process (such as the arrival process) is intermittently
and instantaneously cleared. Various clearing policies have been studied, e.g., clearance when
the input reaches a threshold, or at i.i.d. random times independent of the input process.
Some of our Markovian models (the ones without service) can be regarded as stochastic
clearing models of Poisson arrivals. Similarly, our non‚ÄďMarkovian models without services
can be regarded as models of clearing at renewal arrival times. However, stochastic clearing
models do not generate output dynamics like our models in presence of service.
Models considered in the present paper are also related to the queueing models with disasters
or catastrophes, see, e.g, [20, 6, 21, 11, 18, 36, 3, 12, 23, 35, 34, 7, 31]. We have already
mentioned results on the M/M/1, M/M/2 and M/M/‚ąěqueues in [20, 21, 7], respectively. In
[6], an M/G/1 queue with ‚Äúdisasters‚ÄĚ has been considered, where disasters occur at certain


--- Page 4 ---
4
DONGZHOU HUANG, GUODONG PANG, IZABELLA STUHL, AND YURI SUHOV
random times including (a) deterministic equidistant times, (b) random times independent
of the queueing process, and (c) at crossings of some pre-specified level. In these works,
stationary distributions of the workload processes have been characterized via their Laplace
transforms using certain modifications of the Lindley recursion. The paper [18] considers
an M/M/1 queue with catastrophes, where the server breaks downs at i.i.d. random times,
independent of the service process. At the breakdown times, all jobs are lost, and it takes an
exponential random time to repair the system. See also similar formulations of ‚Äúcatastrophes‚ÄĚ
or ‚Äúclearing‚ÄĚ in [11, 3, 12, 23, 31]. An associated (jump) diffusion approximation has been
considered in [11, 10, 9]. In [15], a computational approach has been developed, for non-
homogeneous Markovian single-server and infinite-server queueing models, whose formulation
is like our Markovian models but with nonstationary transition rates. There are related
works on M/M/1 queues with certain types of abandonment at catastrophes; see, for example,
[36, 12, 35]. However, these models differ from the standard M/M/1+M queue with random
resetting considered here. More general birth-death processes with catastrophes have been
studied in [8, 10]. See also [13] for a CTMC regulated by a point process, and [29] for certain
stochastic processes with random regulations.
Our study complements and provide alternative approaches to the existing literature of
Markovian queues with disasters, while the non‚ÄďMarkovian models with random resetting
at arrival times are completely novel. We also refer the readers to some recent studies of
random walks, Brownian motions and diffusions with random resetting in [14, 30, 32, 1, 22];
in some methodological aspects, they are close to our current work.
In addition to clearing models described above, there are some recent studies of queues
with random resetting in [4, 26], focused on an M/G/1 queue with service times being reset at
random times whenever the service time is longer than a threshold. This concept of stochastic
resetting is also exploited in random search problems, Cf. [5, 24].
Organization of the paper. The paper is organized as follows. The Markovian models with
resetting are studied first, with M/M/‚ąěqueues in Section 2, M/M/r queues in Section
3, and M/M/1+M queues in Section 4. Some additional proofs for M/M/r queues with
resetting are collected in the Appendix, Section 9. The non‚ÄďMarkovian models are studied
next, with GI/GI/1 and GI/GI/r queues with resettings at arrival times in Sections 5 and 6,
respectively, and with infinite-server queues with resettings at arrival times in Section 7.
2. The M/M/‚ąěqueue with random resetting
The standard assumption in Sections 2‚Äď4 is that the jobs arrive in a Poisson process at
rate őĽ ‚Č•0, the services times are i.i.d. exponential of rate ¬Ķ ‚Č•0, and all jobs in the system
are cleared/reset after subsequent i.i.d. exponential random times of rate őļ > 0. The case
of ¬Ķ = 0 means that no jobs are served; in this case we get a stochastic clearing model of a
Poisson process, Cf. [28, 33], and its stationary distribution is known to be geometric with
parameter ŌĪ :=
őĽ
őĽ+őļ, that is, ŌÄi = ŌĪi(1 ‚ąíŌĪ), i ‚Č•0.
This section focuses on an M/M/‚ąěqueue with resetting. Denote by X(t) the number of
jobs in the system at time t ‚Č•0. Then {X(t) : t ‚Č•0} is the continuous-time Markov chain

őļ = 0. We denote by ŌÄ = {ŌÄi}‚ąě

stationary distribution has been recently derived in [7], which starts from deriving the
transient distribution ŌÄi(t) = P(X(t) = i) and then letting t ‚Üí‚ąě. Here we directly derive
the stationary distribution and provide an alternative expression. For simplicity of notation,
we define

With Lemma 2.1, we proceed to check (2.8). Noting that

Remark 2.1. While the stationary distribution of the classical M/M/‚ąěqueue follows a
Poisson distribution, introducing resetting leads to a stationary distribution that remains
‚ÄúPoisson-like‚ÄĚ in a general sense. In fact, it follows a mixed Poisson distribution. Specifically,
suppose that X|Y ‚ąľPoisson(őłY ) and Y has the density ő≥(1 ‚ąíy)ő≥‚ąí1 for 0 < y < 1. Then,
the stationary distribution ŌÄ for the M/M/‚ąěqueue with resetting satisfies
ŌÄn = P(X = n).
Alternatively, the stationary distribution ŌÄ of the M/M/‚ąěqueue with resetting can be
interpreted from a different perspective. Suppose that ő≥ > 0 and őł > 0 and define
C = Cő≥,őł :=
Z 1
0
(1 ‚ąíy)ő≥‚ąí1e‚ąíőły dy.
Furthermore, assume that Y is a random variable with the pdf
1
C (1 ‚ąíy)ő≥‚ąí1e‚ąíőły,
0 < y < 1.
Then, it is straightforward to verify
ŌÄn = Cő≥ őłnE [Y n]
n!
.
2
Remark 2.2. In the special case where ő≥ = 1 (i.e., őļ = ¬Ķ) and őł > 0, we have a more explicit
formula for the stationary distribution. Specifically, it follows by applying integration by
parts for multiple times that
ŌÄn = 1

Remark 2.3. By letting ő≥ ‚Üí0, we observe that our result for the stationary distribution of
the M/M/‚ąěqueue with resetting (i.e., ő≥ > 0 or őļ > 0) is consistent with that of the classical
M/M/‚ąěqueue (i.e., ő≥ = 0 or őļ = 0). Indeed, using integration by parts,
An =

n!e‚ąíőł.
This result is consistent with the formula for the stationary distribution of the classical
M/M/‚ąěqueue, which corresponds to the case ő≥ = 0.
2

our notation, the result in [7, Theorem 5.1] becomes: for n ‚Č•0,

DONGZHOU HUANG, GUODONG PANG, IZABELLA STUHL, AND YURI SUHOV
This completes the proof of the statement.
2
3. The M/M/r queue with random resetting
We study an M/M/r queue under the FCFS discipline with random resetting. Let X(t) be
the number of jobs in the system at time t. Then {X(t) : t ‚Č•0} is again a CTMC on Z+,
with the transition rates given by:
i ‚Č•0
‚Üí
i + 1
rate
őĽ
(arrival),
i ‚Č•1
‚Üí
i ‚ąí1
rate
(i ‚ąßr)¬Ķ
(departure),
i ‚Č•1
‚Üí
0
rate
őļ
(resetting).
(3.1)
Here x ‚ąßy = min{x, y} for x, y ‚ąąR. Note that the CTMC {X(t)} with ¬Ķ > 0 is dominated
by the corresponding process with ¬Ķ = 0, and hence, it has a unique stationary distribution.
In the rest of the section, we assume ¬Ķ > 0, and adopt the notation:
őł := őĽ/¬Ķ,
ő≥ := őļ/¬Ķ.
For the special case r = 2, the stationary distribution is derived in [21] by first studying
the transient behavior ŌÄi(t) = P(X(t) = i) and then letting t ‚Üí‚ąě. The general case r > 2
has not been studied in the literature.
Theorem 3.1. Assume that őĽ > 0, ¬Ķ > 0, and őļ > 0. The stationary distribution ŌÄ for the
M/M/r queue with random resetting is given by the following: for k = 0, 1, . . . , r ‚ąí1,
ŌÄk = őłk
k! (Ak ‚ąíLr‚ąí1,rCk) ,
(3.2)
and for k ‚Č•r,
ŌÄk = őĪk‚ąír+1ŌÄr‚ąí1
= őĪk‚ąír+1őłr‚ąí1
(r ‚ąí1)!
(Ar‚ąí1 ‚ąíLr‚ąí1,rCr‚ąí1) .
(3.3)
Here, őł = őĽ/¬Ķ, ő≥ = őļ/¬Ķ,
őĪ = r + őł + ő≥ ‚ąí
p

Proof. To characterize the stationary distribution, we again use the PBEs:
őĽŌÄ0 = ¬ĶŌÄ1 + őļ
X
j‚Č•1
ŌÄj,
(őĽ + i¬Ķ + őļ)ŌÄi = őĽŌÄi‚ąí1 + (i + 1)¬ĶŌÄi+1,
1 ‚Č§i < r,
(őĽ + r¬Ķ + őļ)ŌÄi = őĽŌÄi‚ąí1 + r¬ĶŌÄi+1,
i ‚Č•r.
(3.8)
Using a similar argument as in the proof of Theorem 4.1, we obtain that a nonnegative
sequence ŌÄ is the stationary distribution if and only it is convergent and satisfies the following
equations:
(őł + ő≥)ŌÄ0 ‚ąíŌÄ1 = ő≥,
(3.9)
(n + 1)ŌÄn+1 ‚ąí(n + őł + ő≥)ŌÄn + őłŌÄn‚ąí1 = 0,
1 ‚Č§n < r,
(3.10)
rŌÄn+1 ‚ąí(r + őł + ő≥)ŌÄn + őłŌÄn‚ąí1 = 0,
n ‚Č•r.
(3.11)
In the rest of the proof, we assume ŌÄ = {ŌÄn}‚ąě
n=0 is the unique stationary distribution and use
the above equations to derive its expression.
Starting from (3.11), it follows that for n ‚Č•r
ŌÄn+1 ‚ąíőĪŌÄn = ő≤(ŌÄn ‚ąíőĪŌÄn‚ąí1),
where
őĪ = r + őł + ő≥ ‚ąí
p

2r
,
ő≤ = r + őł + ő≥ +
p

and hence,
ŌÄn

őĪn (ŌÄr ‚ąíőĪŌÄr‚ąí1).

ŌÄk = ő≤(ŌÄr ‚ąíőĪŌÄr‚ąí1)
ő≤ ‚ąíőĪ
ő≤k‚ąír ‚ąíőĪ(ŌÄr ‚ąíő≤ŌÄr‚ąí1)
ő≤ ‚ąíőĪ
őĪk‚ąír,
for k ‚Č•r + 1. Noting that 0 < őĪ < 1 and ő≤ > 1, the requirement P‚ąě

ő≤(ŌÄr ‚ąíőĪŌÄr‚ąí1)

= 0,

We have expressed ŌÄk in terms of ŌÄr‚ąí1 for k ‚Č•r. It remains to derive expressions for
ŌÄ0, ŌÄ1, . . . , ŌÄr‚ąí1. To this end, we define the following truncated generating function:

Before proving (3.20), we pause to establish the following formula:
őłr(Ar‚ąí1Cr ‚ąíCr‚ąí1Ar) = ő≥(r ‚ąí1)!,
(3.21)
for r = 1, 2 . . . , when őł > 0. Indeed, by applying the recursive formulas for Ak in (2.10) and

=

=

=
(r ‚ąí1)őłr‚ąí1(Ar‚ąí2Cr‚ąí1 ‚ąíCr‚ąí2Ar‚ąí1).
Furthermore, we can also verify that őł(A0C1 ‚ąíC0A1) = ő≥. By combining these two results,
we directly obtain that őłr(Ar‚ąí1Cr ‚ąíCr‚ąí1Ar) = ő≥(r ‚ąí1)!. We now turn to (3.20). It follows
by (3.21) that
ő≥ +
őłr‚ąí1
(r ‚ąí1)!Cr‚ąí1 (őłAr ‚ąírőĪAr‚ąí1)
=
őłr
(r ‚ąí1)! (Ar‚ąí1Cr ‚ąíCr‚ąí1Ar) +
őłr‚ąí1
(r ‚ąí1)!Cr‚ąí1 (őłAr ‚ąírőĪAr‚ąí1)
=
őłr‚ąí1
(r ‚ąí1)!Ar‚ąí1 (őłCr ‚ąírőĪCr‚ąí1) .
(3.22)
Therefore, it is immediate that
őłr‚ąí1
(r‚ąí1)!Ar‚ąí1 (őłAr ‚ąírőĪAr‚ąí1)
ő≥ +
őłr‚ąí1
(r‚ąí1)!Cr‚ąí1 (őłAr ‚ąírőĪAr‚ąí1) =
őłr‚ąí1
(r‚ąí1)!Ar‚ąí1 (őłAr ‚ąírőĪAr‚ąí1)
őłr‚ąí1
(r‚ąí1)!Ar‚ąí1 (őłCr ‚ąírőĪCr‚ąí1) = őłAr ‚ąírőĪAr‚ąí1
őłCr ‚ąírőĪCr‚ąí1
= Lr‚ąí1,r.
Finally, recalling that ŌÄn = őĪn‚ąí(r‚ąí1)ŌÄr‚ąí1 for n ‚Č•r + 1, the final expression of ŌÄk for k ‚Č•r
follows directly from (3.2). Additionally, we also verify in Lemma 3.3 that the sequence
ŌÄk defined in (3.2) and (3.3) is always nonnegative and convergent. We thus conclude the
proof.
‚Ė°
The proofs for the next three lemmas are relegated to the appendix.
Lemma 3.1. For k = 1, . . . , r ‚ąí1,
őłBk+1,r ‚ąí(k + őł + ő≥)Bk,r + kBk‚ąí1,r = 0,
(3.23)
and for k = 1, . . . , r ‚ąí2,
őłBk+1,r‚ąí1 ‚ąí(k + őł + ő≥)Bk,r‚ąí1 + kBk‚ąí1,r‚ąí1 = 0.
(3.24)

Bk,r = Ar
ő≥ Ck,
(3.25)
where Ak is given in (3.5) and Ck is given in (3.6). Similarly, for k = 0, 1, . . . , r ‚ąí1,

Ck.
(3.26)

(3.27)
Lemma 3.3. The sequence {ŌÄk} defined in (3.2) and (3.3) is always nonnegative and
convergent.
Remark 3.1. Using the recursive formulas for An in (2.10) and Cn in (3.27), we can directly
verify that the expressions for ŌÄ defined in (3.2) and (3.3) satisfies (3.9), (3.10), and (3.11).
We begin with (3.11). Noting that ŌÄk = őĪk‚ąír+1ŌÄr‚ąí1 for k ‚Č•r ‚ąí1, it follows by a direct
calculation that
rŌÄn+1 ‚ąí(r + őł + ő≥)ŌÄn + őłŌÄn‚ąí1 = őĪn‚ąírŌÄr‚ąí1
␀rőĪ2 ‚ąí(r + őł + ő≥)őĪ + őł

= 0.
We then turn to (3.9). This is equivalent to
(őł + ő≥) (A0 ‚ąíLr‚ąí1,rC0) ‚ąíőł (A1 ‚ąíLr‚ąí1,rC1) = ő≥.
Comparing the last display with (2.9), it suffices to show that
Lr‚ąí1,r ((őł + ő≥)C0 ‚ąíőłC1) = 0.
Noting that C0 = 1 and C1 = 1 + ő≥/őł, the above equation holds directly.
Subsequently, we verify (3.10). For n = 1, 2, . . . , r ‚ąí2, we have
(n + 1)ŌÄn+1 ‚ąí(n + őł + ő≥)ŌÄn + őłŌÄn‚ąí1

By letting ő≥ ‚Üí0, we show that our result for the stationary distribution of the M/M/r

4. M/M/1+M queues with random resetting
In this section, we consider the M/M/1+M queue with random resetting under the FCFS
discipline, where the patience times are i.i.d. exponentially distributed of rate őĹ, independent
from the arrival, service and and resetting processes. Let X(t) denote the number of jobs
in the system at time t ‚Č•0. Then the process {X(t) : t ‚Č•0} is a continuous-time Markov
chain taking values in Z+ = {0, 1, 2, . . . } with the following transition rates:
i ‚Č•0
‚Üí
i + 1,
rate
őĽ
(arrival),
i ‚Č•1
‚Üí
i ‚ąí1,
rate
¬Ķ + (i ‚ąí1)őĹ
(departure),
i ‚Č•1
‚Üí
0,
rate
őļ
(resetting).
When őĹ = 0, the process {X(t) : t ‚Č•0} reduces to that in an M/M/1 queue with random
resetting under the FCFS discipline. The CTMC {X(t) : t ‚Č•0} is positive recurrent for ‚ąÄ
őĽ, őļ > 0. Owing to the dominance, we obtain the following property: For any ¬Ķ ‚Č•0, őĽ ‚Č•0,
and őļ > 0, the CTMC {X(t) : t ‚Č•0} is positive recurrent and has a unique stationary
distribution. The stationary distribution ŌÄ = {ŌÄi : i ‚ąąZ+} has been derived in [20], by first
studying the transient distribution ŌÄi(t) = P(X(t) = i) and then letting t ‚Üí‚ąě. Following
our approach for the case with reneging, from the PBEs, one can easily obtain that result.
For completeness, we state the result below.
Theorem 4.1. Assume that őĹ = 0, and either ¬Ķ > 0 and őļ > 0, or őļ = 0 and ¬Ķ > őĽ ‚Č•0.
The stationary distribution ŌÄ of the M/M/1 queue with resetting under the FCFS discipline
is given by
ŌÄi = ŌĀi(1 ‚ąíŌĀ),
i = 0, 1, 2, . . . ,
where ŌĀ ‚ąą(0, 1) is given by
ŌĀ = 1
2

őĽ/¬Ķ + őļ/¬Ķ + 1 ‚ąí
p
(őĽ/¬Ķ + őļ/¬Ķ + 1)2 ‚ąí4őĽ/¬Ķ

.
(4.1)
We proceed to study the M/M/1+M queue with random resetting. It suffices to focus on
the case where őĹ > 0. Additionally, we also assume that ¬Ķ > 0 and őļ > 0. Note that the
process X(t) with őĹ > 0 is dominated by the process with őĹ = 0. Based on this relation,
we can conclude that the stationary distribution of X(t) exists and is unique. Furthermore,
when ¬Ķ = őĹ, the transition rates of X(t) coincide with those of an M/M/‚ąěqueue, implying
that the dynamics of the two systems are identical. Consequently, the stationary distribution
of the M/M/‚ąěqueue provides useful insights for deriving the stationary distribution of the
M/M/1+M queue with random resetting.
Before presenting our main result for the stationary distribution of the M/M/1+M queue
with resetting, we pause to define necessary notation. Define
őł := őĽ
őĹ ,
ő≥ := őļ
őĹ ,
and
ő∑ := ¬Ķ
őĹ .
(4.2)
Theorem 4.2. Assume that ¬Ķ, őĹ, őļ > 0. The stationary distribution ŌÄ for the M/M/1+M
queue with random resetting under the FCFS discipline is given by the following: for k =

(PBEs):
őĽŌÄ0 = ¬ĶŌÄ1 + őļ

for k = 1, 2, . . .

(őł + ő≥)ŌÄ0 ‚ąíő∑ŌÄ1 = ő≥,
(4.4)

Remark 4.1. When ¬Ķ = őĹ, it follows that ő∑ = 1. It is straightforward to verify that
the stationary distribution of the M/M/1+M queue with random resetting, given in (4.3),
coincides with that of the M/M/‚ąěqueue with random resetting in (2.2).

given in (4.3) converges to that of the classical M/M/1+M queue (i.e., when ő≥ = 0 or őļ = 0).

2
5. The GI/GI/1 queues with random resetting at arrival times
In this section, we consider a GI/GI/1 queue under the FCFS discipline with random
resetting at arrival times, particularly, focusing on the waiting times (delays) of jobs in the
system. We will be using [2] as a main reference book; the original works containing related
results can be traced via comments and the bibliography in [2].
5.1. The Lindley recursion for GI/GI/1 queue. The key ingredient of the GI/GI/1 model is
a sequence of real random variables (RVs) Xn, n ‚Č•0, where Xn = Vn ‚ąíUn, Un is the nth
inter-arrival time and Vn the nth service time. It is assumed that these RVs Xn are IID, with
a common cumulative distribution function (CDF) FX. In all of Sections 5‚Äď7, we assume
that FX is a proper CDF on R. The latter signifies that
lim
x‚Üí‚ąí‚ąěFX(x) = 0, lim
x‚Üí‚ąěFX(x) = 1,
i.e., that the RVs Xn take only finite values.
Let Wn be the nth waiting time. The Lindley recursive equation (originated in [19]) states:
Wn+1 = (Wn + Xn)+,
n ‚Č•0,
(5.1)
with some given initial RV W0 ‚Č•0, assumed to be independent of {Xn}. Here and below, we
set Y + = 0‚ą®Y . Then {Wn, n ‚Č•0} is a discrete-time Markov chain (DTMC) on R+ = [0, ‚ąě).
It is known (see, e.g., [2, Chapter X.1]) that if E[X] < 0, there exists a unique stationary
distribution of DTMC {Wn}. (In fact, for E[X] < 0, the DTMC {Wn} is Harris ergodic.)
The stationary distribution is characterized by a proper CDF FW on R+ determined as a
unique solution to the stationary Wiener‚ÄďHopf (WH) equation
FW(t) = (FX ‚ąóFW)(t)1R+(t),
t ‚ąąR.
(5.2)


--- Page 22 ---
22
DONGZHOU HUANG, GUODONG PANG, IZABELLA STUHL, AND YURI SUHOV
Here and below, G1 ‚ąóG2 means the convolution of CDFs Gi:
G1 ‚ąóG2(t) =
Z
R
G1(t ‚ąíy)dG2(y) = G2 ‚ąóG1(t),
t ‚ąąR.
When E[X] ‚Č•0, (5.2) has no solution among proper CDFs (again, see [2, Chapter X.1]).
A stochastic version of equation (5.2) reads
W
d= (W + X)+ .
(5.3)
Here X and W are ‚Äėgeneric‚Äô RVs with CDFs FX and FW, respectively, independent of each
other, and
d= means ‚Äúequality in distribution‚ÄĚ.
5.2. The modified Lindley recursion for a GI/GI/1 queue with resetting. We consider a
GI/GI/1 model where random resettings occur independently at arrival times. That is, the
(n + 1)st reset waiting time W R
n+1 either continues as in (5.1) with probability q ‚ąą(0, 1), or is
set to be 0 with probability 1 ‚ąíq, independently of (Xk, W R
k ) with 0 ‚Č§k ‚Č§n. Recursively, it
can be expressed as follows:
W R
n+1 = Zn+1(W R
n + Xn)+,
n ‚Č•0.
(5.4)
Here {Zn : n ‚Č•1} is a sequence of IID Bernoulli RVs with probability P(Zn = 0) = q =
1 ‚ąíP(Zn = 1), independent of {Xn}. Equivalently, we can write
W R
n+1 =
(
0,
with probability q,
(W R
n + Xn)+,
with probability 1 ‚ąíq,
(5.5)
independently of (Xk, W R
k ) with 0 ‚Č§k ‚Č§n.
Equations (5.4) and (5.5) are referred to as a modified Lindley recursion with resetting.
The sequence {W R
n} forms a DTMC on R+. We show that it is Harris ergodic. This implies
that the DTMC {W R
n} has a unique stationary distribution, and that the corresponding CDF,
denoted by FW R, is proper on R+ and satisfies FW R(0) > 0.
Proposition 5.1. For any q ‚ąą(0, 1) and a sequence of IID RVs {Xn}, the DTMC {W R
n}
is Harris ergodic.
Proof. Define T = inf{n ‚Č•0 : W R
n = 0}, i.e., T is the first hitting time of 0.
It is
straightforward that for n ‚Č•1,
P(T > n) ‚Č§P (Z1 Őł= 0, ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ , Zn Őł= 0) = (1 ‚ąíq)n.
Therefore,
E [T] =
‚ąě
X
n=0
P(T > n) ‚Č§1
q < ‚ąě.
Hence, the time for the chain to return to state 0 has a finite mean, and therefore the state 0
forms a regeneration set. Then, Harris ergodicity is straightforward.
Observe that the process is regenerative (possibly after the first cycle in case the system
starts from W R
0 > 0), with the cycles (W R
1 , . . . , W R
T) where W R
1 = 0 and W R
T for T being


--- Page 23 ---
23
geometric of parameter q. That is, the state 0 forms a regeneration set. Hence, the time for
the chain to return to state 0 has a finite mean. Then, Harris ergodicity is straightforward.
‚Ė°
The stationary CDF FW R is identified as a solution to a stationary WH equation with
resetting
FW R(t) =
h
q + (1 ‚ąíq)(FW R ‚ąóFX)(t)
i
1R+(t),
t ‚ąąR,
(5.6)
or to its stochastic analog
W R d= Z(W R + X)+ .
(5.7)
Here X and W R are ‚Äėgeneric‚Äô RVs with CDFs FX and FW R, respectively, and Z is a Bernoulli
RV with P(Z = 0) = q = 1‚ąíP(Z = 1). Furthermore, the RVs X, W R and Z are independent,
and, as before,
d= means ‚Äúequality in distribution‚ÄĚ.
5.3. The operator calculus for a GI/GI/1 queue with resetting. To study the stationary
distribution of the GI/GI/1 queue with resetting, it is useful to introduce the following
operator concepts. We define F to be the space of all proper CDFs. It is well-known that F is
closed under convolution and is convex with respect to addition, where addition is understood
as pointwise addition of CDFs. Furthermore, we define an operator K from F to F by
K :
F
‚ąí‚Üí
F
H(x)
7‚Üí
(H ‚ąóFX)(x) 1R+(x).
It is straightforward to show that (H ‚ąóFX)(x) 1R+(x) is a proper CDF, and therefore K is a
valid operator on F. Additionally, we can prove that K is convex-combination preserving, in
the sense that
K

by I the identity operator on F, that is, I maps each proper CDF to itself. Additionally, we
define Kn to be the n-fold composition of K; in particular, K0 = I.
With the above preparation, we proceed to derive the stationary distribution by solving
for a proper CDF H(x) satisfying
H(t) =

1R+(t),
t ‚ąąR.
In terms of the operator K, this equation is equivalent to
H = q1R+ + (1 ‚ąíq)K (H) .
(5.9)

(5.10)

since Kj ␀1R+



.
In summary, the series (5.10) defines a proper CDF and provides a solution to (5.9). Further-
more, it follows by (5.6) that FW R also satisfies (5.9). If it can be shown that (5.9) admits a
unique bounded solution, then we may conclude that FW R coincides with the series (5.10),
thereby yielding an explicit expression for the CDF of the stationary distribution. The
preceding deduction is summarized in the following theorem.
Theorem 5.1. For any q ‚ąą(0, 1), the equation (5.9) has a unique bounded solution. Since
both FW R and the CDF defined by the series (5.10) satisfy (5.9), they must coincide. That is,
FW R =
‚ąě
X
j=0
q(1 ‚ąíq)jKj ␀1R+


=q1R+ + q(1 ‚ąíq)FX1R+ + q(1 ‚ąíq)2(FX ‚ąó(FX1R+))1R+
+ q(1 ‚ąíq)3(FX ‚ąó((FX ‚ąó(FX1R+))1R+))1R+ + ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ .
(5.11)
Proof. We only need to check uniqueness. Let G1 : R ‚ÜíR and G2 : R ‚ÜíR be two bounded
functions such that Gi(t) =
h

i

G(t) = (1 ‚ąíq)(G ‚ąóFX)(t)1R+(t),
t ‚ąąR.

supt‚ąąR |G(t)|. If őď > 0, then
őď = sup

This is a contradiction, since 1 ‚ąíq < 1. Therefore, őď = 0, which implies G ‚Č°0.
‚Ė°
We now turn to the case where RV X ‚Č•0. In this case, the CDF FX(x) is positive only if
x ‚Č•0. Therefore, FX1R+ = FX. Then, FW R determined by the series in (5.11) reduces to
FW R = q1R+ + q(1 ‚ąíq)FX + q(1 ‚ąíq)2FX ‚ąóFX + q(1 ‚ąíq)3FX ‚ąóFX ‚ąóFX + ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ .
(5.12)
Let Ōē(őł) = E[eiőłX] and Ōą(őł) = E[eiőłW R] be the characteristic functions (CFs) of X and W R,
respectively, for őł ‚ąąR. Then (5.12) is equivalent to
Ōą(őł) = q
‚ąě
P
k=0
(1 ‚ąíq)k(Ōē(őł))k =
q
1 ‚ąí(1 ‚ąíq)Ōē(őł).
(5.13)
We obtain the following assertion.
Theorem 5.2. For any q ‚ąą(0, 1) and RV X ‚Č•0, the RV W R, corresponding to the
stationary distribution of the GI/GI/1 queue with resetting, is a proper RV with values in
[0, ‚ąě). Furthermore, its CDF FW R and CF Ōą are given by the expressions in (5.12) and
(5.13).
As an example, we next consider the case X taking integer values.
Example 5.1. We start with the simplest scenario: X = 1. For simplicity, suppose that
W0 = 0. In the model without resetting, the waiting time Wn = n ‚Üí‚ąěas n ‚Üí‚ąě. Cf. (5.1).
In the presence of resetting, we have a DTMC {W R
n} on the state space Z+, with transition
probabilities P = (Pij): Pi,0 = q and Pi,i+1 = 1 ‚ąíq for i ‚Č•0. The stationarity condition
ŌÄP = ŌÄ gives q P
i‚Č•1
ŌÄi = ŌÄ0 and (1 ‚ąíq)ŌÄi = ŌÄi+1 for i ‚Č•0. Thus, we obtain that ŌÄi = q(1 ‚ąíq)i,


--- Page 26 ---
26
DONGZHOU HUANG, GUODONG PANG, IZABELLA STUHL, AND YURI SUHOV
i ‚Č•0, i.e., the RV W R is geometric. It is straightforward that the CDF FW R and CF Ōą
satisfy (5.12) and (5.13).
Example 5.2. Next, assume that X takes values k = 1, 2, . . ., with probabilities pk and
take again W0 = 0.
Then the DTMC {W R
n} has transition probabilities Pi,0 = q for
i ‚Č•0, and Pi,j = (1 ‚ąíq)pj‚ąíi for i ‚Č•0 and j ‚Č•i + 1; the remaining entries equal to
zero. Let ŌÄ = (ŌÄ0, ŌÄ1, . . . ) be the stationary distribution. Then we get q
‚ąě
P
i=0
ŌÄi = ŌÄ0, and
(1 ‚ąíq)
i‚ąí1
P
j=0
ŌÄjpi‚ąíj = ŌÄi for i ‚Č•1. From this we obtain the equation for the characteristic
functions: Ōą(őł) = 1 + (1 ‚ąíq)Ōą(őł)Ōē(őł), which results in (5.13).
6. The GI/GI/r queue with random resetting at arrival times
6.1. The Kiefer‚ÄďWolfowitz recursion for a GI/GI/r queue. In a standard GI/GI/r queue
with r > 1 servers and under the FCFS discipline, we operate with a collection of random
vectors {W n} where W n = (Wn1, . . . , Wnr) and 0 ‚Č§Wn1 ‚Č§. . . ‚Č§Wnr. In other words, W n
takes values in the simplex S+
‚Č§‚äāRr where S+
‚Č§=
n
x = (x1, . . . , xr) : 0 ‚Č§x1 ‚Č§. . . ‚Č§xr
o
.
Pictorially, W n represents the residual workload vector at the time of arrival of the nth job,
and its smallest entry, Wn1, gives the waiting time for the nth job.
The recursion that generates the sequence {W n} is due to Kiefer and Wolfowitz [17]:
W n+1 =
h
R
␀W n + Vn e(1)
‚ąíUn 1
i+
(6.1)
with the following ingredients on the RHS:
(i) Vn is the service time of the nth arrival, and Un is the time between the nth and
(n + 1)st arrival. It is assumed that the pairs (Un, Vn), n = 0, 1, . . ., form an IID
sequence. The joint CDF for (Un, Vn) is denoted by G:
G(u, v) = P(Un ‚Č§u, Vn ‚Č§v).
(6.2)
We will assume that CDF G is proper, i.e., RVs Un and Vn take finite values only.
(ii) e(1) = (1, 0, . . . , 0) ‚ąąZr
+ and 1 = (1, . . . , 1) ‚ąąZr
+ are r-dimensional 0, 1-vectors.
(iii) R
␀W n + Vn e(1)
‚ąąS+
‚Č§is the result of the re-arrangement operation R applied to
the vector W n + Vn e(1) ‚ąąRr
+: the vector R
␀W n + Vn e(1)
has the same collection of
entries as W n + Vn e(1) re-arranged in the non-decreasing order.
(iv)
h
R
␀W n + Vn e(1)
‚ąíUn 1
i+
‚ąąS+
‚Č§is the vector obtained when the negative entries
in R
␀W n + Vn e(1)
‚ąíUk 1
are replaced with zeros and non-negative entries are left
intact.


--- Page 27 ---
27
Equation (6.1) generates a DTMC {W n, n = 0, 1, . . .} on S+
‚Č§. It can be re-written in terms
of the r-dimensional CDFs Fn(x) = P(W n ‚Č§x), n ‚Č•0, as follows:
Fn+1(x) =

Fn+1 = KFn
where operator K acts on a CDF H by
(KH)(x) =

(6.5)
It is known that if the traffic intensity ŌĀ := E[V ]

equation
W

or, equivalently, F = KF, i.e.,
F(x) =

Sr 1(w ‚ąąA(x, u, v))dF(w)dG(u, v)
(6.6)
has a unique solution giving a proper CDF F on Rr. In fact, for ŌĀ < 1, the DTMC {W n} is
Harris ergodic. On the other hand, when ŌĀ ‚Č•1, there is no proper CDF F on Rr satisfying
equation (6.6), Cf. [2, Chapter XII.2].
6.2. The modified Kiefer‚ÄďWolfowitz recursion for a GI/GI/r queue with resetting. The
model with random resetting at arrival times again involves the parameter q ‚ąą(0, 1). Set
0 = (0, . . . , 0). Equations (6.1) and (6.3) are replaced with

(6.8)

{W R
n, n = 0, 1, . . .} on S+
‚Č§. Again, the vector W R
n represents the residual workloads at the
servers at the nth arrival time.

Accordingly, the stationary equations for {W R

(i)

‚Č§+ (1 ‚ąíq)KF R

‚Č§
solved by
(iv)
F R = q
␀I ‚ąí(1 ‚ąíq)K
‚ąí11S+
‚Č§
= q1S+
‚Č§+ q(1 ‚ąíq)K1S+
‚Č§+ q(1 ‚ąíq)2K21S+
‚Č§+ . . . .
(6.9)
Proposition 6.1. For any q ‚ąą(0, 1) and a sequence of
IID
RV pairs
{(Un, Vn)}, the
DTMC {W R
n} is Harris ergodic.
Proof. We will only give here a sketch of the (rather tedious) proof as it does not contain
serious novel elements. It is reduced to a repetition of arguments from [2, Chapters XII.1,
XII.2]. The crux of the matter is Theorem 1.2 on page 432 in [2, Chapter XII.1] and Theorem
2.2 on page 345 in [2, Chapter XII.2] rewritten in a modified form for the DTMC with
resetting {W R
n}. In turn, the proof of the modified theorems is based on analogs of Lemma
1.3 and Lemmas 2.3 and 2.4 in [2, Chapters XII.1, XII.2]. Such analogs connect the DTMC
{W R
n} with the majorizing Markov chain {f
W R
n} where arriving jobs are directed to servers in
the cyclic order with probability 1 ‚ąíq and trigger resetting of the whole vector of residual
workloads to 0 with probability q. The analysis of the majorizing DTMC {f
W R
n} is essentially
reduced to the GI/GI/1 model with resetting which leads to the assertion of Proposition
6.1.
‚Ė°
The above construction then leads to the following result.
Theorem 6.1. For any q ‚ąą(0, 1), the series in (6.9)(iv) determines a proper CDF satisfying
(6.9)(ii). Furthermore, equation (6.9)(ii) has a unique bounded solution, and this solution is
given by the series in (6.9)(iv).
Proof. As in the case of the model GI/GI/1 with resetting, the fact that the series in (6.9)(iv)
gives a solution to (6.9)(ii) follows from the construction with the help of Proposition 6.1.
Uniqueness is also established by the same argument as for the GI/GI/1 model.
‚Ė°
7. The GI/GI/‚ąěqueue with random resetting at arrival times
7.1. The recursion for a GI/GI/‚ąěqueue. In the section, we study GI/GI/‚ąěqueue, where
infinitely many servers are available. A standard GI/GI/‚ąěcan be described via a sequence
of random-dimension random vectors W n = (Wn1, . . . , WnS(n)), n = 0, 1, . . . . Specifically,
S(n) is a random variable with nonnegative integer values, which determines the dimension


--- Page 29 ---
29
of W n and corresponds to the number of active servers when the nth job arrives. If S(n) ‚Č•1,
the entries of W n satisfy that Wn1 ‚Č•. . . ‚Č•WnS(n) > 0, and they correspond to the residual
workloads of the active servers. In particular, the largest entry, Wn1, gives the time needed
for clearing the system of jobs entered before the nth arrival time. If S(n) = 0, then W n
reduces to E, meaning the nth job finds an empty queue at its arrival. With the above
definition, it is straightforward to see that W n takes values in the union O+(‚Č•) :=
‚ąě‚ą™
s=0 Os
+(‚Č•)
of simplexes Os
+(‚Č•) =

x = (x1, . . . , xs) : x1 ‚Č•. . . ‚Č•xs > 0
	
of varying dimension s ‚Č•1,
and a single-state set O0
+(‚Č•) = {E} for s = 0.
The recursion for the residual workload vector {W n} in the GI/GI/‚ąěmodel is given by
W n+1 = R

S
n
P(Vn, W n) ‚ąíUn 1 S(n)+1
+o 
,
n = 0, 1, . . . .
(7.1)
Here the RHS contains the following components:
(i) As in Section 6, Vn is the service time of the nth arrival, and Un is the time between
the nth and (n + 1)st arrival. It is assumed that the pairs (Un, Vn), n = 0, 1, . . ., form
an IID sequence. The joint CDF for (Un, Vn) is again denoted by G and assumed to
be proper.
(ii) S(n) is the dimension of W n and the vector 1 S(n)+1 = (1, . . . , 1) ‚ąąZS(n)+1
+
has all
entries 1.
(iii) P(Vn, W n) ‚ąąRS(n)+1 is the result of concatenation of the value Vn and the vector
W n.
(iv)

P(Vn, W n)‚ąíUn 1 S(n)+1
+ is the vector obtained from P(Vn, W n)‚ąíUn 1 S(n)+1 when
negative entries are replaced with zeros and non-negative entries are left intact.
(v) S
n
P(Vn, W n) ‚ąíUn 1 S(n)+1
+o
is the result of shortening vector

P(Vn, W n) ‚ąí
Un 1 S(n)+1
+ by removing the zero entries. If all entries are removed, it is denoted by
E. Furthermore, the dimension of S
n
P(Vn, W n) ‚ąíUn 1 S(n)+1
+o
equals S(n + 1).
(iv) R

S
n
P(Vn, W n) ‚ąíUn 1 S(n)+1

+
o 
is the result of the re-arrangement applied to
the vector S
n
P(Vn, W n) ‚ąíUn 1 S(n)+1
+o
. Indeed, it has the same collection of
entries as S
n
P(Vn, W n) ‚ąíUn 1 S(n)+1
+o
re-arranged in the non-increasing order.
Equation (7.1) generates a DTMC {W n, n = 0, 1, . . .} on O+(‚Č•).
The probability
distribution of W n on O+(‚Č•) is described by a sequence F n = (Fn,0, Fn,1, Fn,2, . . .) where
Fn,0 = P

7.2. The recursion for a GI/GI/‚ąěqueue with resetting. The recursion for a GI/GI/‚ąěmodel
with resetting takes the form
W R

with probability q,

n
P(Vn, W n) ‚ąíUn 1 S(n)+1
+o 
,
with probability 1 ‚ąíq.

It generates a DTMC {W R
n, n = 0, 1, . . .} on O+(‚Č•).


--- Page 31 ---
31
As above, we rewrite equation (7.9) in terms of the sequence of functions F R
n = (F R
n,0, F R
n,1, . . .),
where
F R
n,0 = P

W R
n = E

,
and for k ‚Č•1, F R
n,k : Ok
+(‚Č•) ‚Üí[0, 1] is a nondecreasing, right-continuous function such that
F R
n,k(x(k)) = P

W R
n ‚ąąOk
+(‚Č•), W n ‚Č§x(k)
.
Denote by e (1) = (1, 0, 0, . . . ) the infinite-dimensional vector whose first entry is 1 and all
remaining entries are 0. Then, we have
F R
n+1 = q e (1) + (1 ‚ąíq) K F R
n,
or, entry-wise,
F R
n+1,0 = q + (1 ‚ąíq)
‚ąě
X
l=0
K0,lF R
n,l,
F R
n+1,k(x(k)) = (1 ‚ąíq)
‚ąě
X
l=k‚ąí1
(Kk,lF R
n,l)(x(k)), x(k) ‚ąąOk
+(‚Č•),
k = 1, 2, . . . .
Therefore, the stationary version becomes
F R = q e (1) + (1 ‚ąíq) K F R ‚áź‚áí( I ‚ąí(1 ‚ąíq) K )F R = q e (1).
(7.10)
Equation (7.10) can be solved by
F R = q

e (1) + (1 ‚ąíq) K e (1) + (1 ‚ąíq)2 K 2 e (1) + ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑

.
(7.11)
Therefore we conclude the following result.
Theorem 7.1. The DTMC {W R
n} has a stationary probability distribution characterized by
equation (7.11).
8. Concluding Remarks
In this paper, we have considered the standard queueing models with random resettings.
Several extensions are possible future works. First, an immediate extension would be to
consider more general Markov chains with random resettings. It would be interesting to
identify conditions under which an explicit stationary distribution could be derived. Some
efforts in this direction are made in our forthcoming paper [25]. Second, for non‚ÄďMarkovian
queues, it would be interesting to consider different forms of resettings other than those
at arrival times. Third, diffusions have been established to approximate the performances
of queues in heavy traffic. It would be also worth considering such diffusion models with
random resetting, particularly, their ergodic properties and characterization of stationary
distributions.
Acknowledgements. G. Pang is partly supported by NSF grants DMS 2216765 and
CMMI 2452849. I. Stuhl and Y. Suhov thank Math Dept, Penn State University for support.
Y. Suhov thanks DPMMS, University of Cambridge, and St John‚Äôs College, Cambridge, for
support. Y. Suhov thanks IHES, Bures-sur-Yvette, for hospitality during a visit in 2024.


--- Page 32 ---
32
DONGZHOU HUANG, GUODONG PANG, IZABELLA STUHL, AND YURI SUHOV

9. Appendix: Additional Proofs for the M/M/r Queue with Resetting
The appendix is devoted to the proofs of Lemmas 3.1, 3.2 and 3.3, and Corollary 3.1.
We begin with the proof of Lemma 3.1.

őłBk+1,r ‚ąí(k + őł + ő≥)Bk,r + kBk‚ąí1,r = 0.

‚Ė°
We then proceed to prove Lemma 3.2.

We proceed to establish (3.25) and (3.26). By symmetry, it suffices to prove (3.25), that is,
Bk,r = Ar
ő≥ Ck,
for k = 0, 1, . . . , r,


--- Page 39 ---
39
and (3.26) follows in a similar manner. The outline of the proof for (3.25) is as follows. We
first verify that the above equality holds for k = 0, 1. Subsequently, we show that ArCk/ő≥
satisfies the same recursion as Bk,r (i.e., (3.23)). Combining these two results establishes
(3.25) for k = 0, 1, . . . , r.
When k = 0, it follows directly from the expression of Bk,r in (3.16) and the expression of
Ak in (2.3) that

Therefore, (3.25) holds when k = 0, 1. Then, it remains to prove that ArCk/ő≥ satisfies the

őłAr
ő≥ Ck+1 ‚ąí(k + őł + ő≥)Ar
ő≥ Ck + kAr
ő≥ Ck‚ąí1 = 0,
which is equivalent to
Ar
ő≥ (őłCk+1 ‚ąí(k + őł + ő≥)Ck + kCk‚ąí1) = 0.
Applying (3.27), the above equation holds, and thus, we complete the proof of (3.25).
‚Ė°
We next prove Lemma 3.3.
Proof of Lemma 3.3. We first show that the sequence ŌÄk defined in (3.2) and (3.3) is con-
vergent. Note that ŌÄk = őĪk‚ąír+1ŌÄr‚ąí1 for k = r, r + 1, . . . . It is immediate to check that
0 < őĪ < 1 when őł, ő≥ > 0. Therefore, to prove the convergence, it suffices to prove that
ŌÄ0, ŌÄ1, . . . , ŌÄr‚ąí1 are finite. This is equivalent to prove Lr‚ąí1,r is finite, since Ak and Ck are
finite for k = 0, 1, . . . . Recalling the definition of Lr‚ąí1,r in (3.7), we only need to prove
őłCr ‚ąírőĪCr‚ąí1 > 0.
(9.4)

When őł > 0, it is straightforward to verify that őł > rőĪ > 0 and

r

Therefore, (9.4) follows directly.
Next, we prove that the ŌÄk defined in (3.2) and (3.3) are always nonnegative. It suffices to
show that
Ak ‚ąíLr‚ąí1,rCk ‚Č•0
for k = 0, 1, . . . , r ‚ąí1.
To simplify the notation, we define Dk := Ak ‚ąíLr‚ąí1,rCk for k = 0, 1, . . . . Then, by the
expression of Lr‚ąí1,r in (3.7), we obtain
Dk = Ak ‚ąíőłAr ‚ąírőĪAr‚ąí1
őłCr ‚ąírőĪCr‚ąí1
Ck = őłAkCr ‚ąíőłCkAr ‚ąírőĪAkCr‚ąí1 + rőĪCkAr‚ąí1
őłCr ‚ąírőĪCr‚ąí1
.
In particular, when k = r,
Dr = rőĪ(Ar‚ąí1Cr ‚ąíCr‚ąí1Ar)
őłCr ‚ąírőĪCr‚ąí1
,
and when k = r ‚ąí1,
Dr‚ąí1 = őł(Ar‚ąí1Cr ‚ąíCr‚ąí1Ar)
őłCr ‚ąírőĪCr‚ąí1
.
Then, the positivity of Dr and Dr‚ąí1 follows from (3.21) and (9.4). Indeed, we have Dr‚ąí1 >
Dr > 0, since őł > rőĪ. Using the recursive formulas for Ak and Ck, we obtain that Dk satisfies
the same recursive formula, that is,
őłDk+1 ‚ąí(k + őł + ő≥)Dk + kDk‚ąí1 = 0,
for k = 1, 2, . . . .
Then, by mathematical induction, we can show that Dk‚ąí1 > Dk > 0 for k = 1, 2, . . . , r.
Indeed, if Dn‚ąí1 > Dn > 0, then
(n ‚ąí1)(Dn‚ąí2 ‚ąíDn‚ąí1) = őł(Dn‚ąí1 ‚ąíDn) + ő≥Dn‚ąí1 > 0,
which gives Dn‚ąí2 > Dn‚ąí1 > 0. Thus, we conclude the nonnegativity of ŌÄk.
‚Ė°
Finally, we prove Corollary 3.1.


--- Page 41 ---
41
Proof of Corollary 3.1. Note that Ak, Ck, őĪ, and Lr‚ąí1,r, which enter the expressions for ŌÄk
in (3.2), and (3.3), all depend on ő≥. Therefore, we need to determine their limits as ő≥ ‚Üí0.
In the remainder of the proof, we use o(1) to denote a quantity that converges to 0 as ő≥ ‚Üí0.

Ak = e‚ąíőł + o(1),
